title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption:
Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average

Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.

This line chart tracks the monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic, measured in millions of square kilometers, across five distinct decadal periods ranging from the 1980s to the 2020s, alongside a specific data series for the year 2025. The horizontal axis spans the twelve months of the year, while the vertical axis quantifies ice coverage from 4 million to 16 million square kilometers. The data reveals a consistent seasonal cycle where ice extent peaks in the early months and reaches a minimum in late summer, yet the magnitude of this coverage has shifted downward over time. The 1980s series, depicted in the lightest blue, maintains the highest values throughout the year, starting near 14.5 million square kilometers in January and bottoming out around 7.5 million square kilometers in September. In contrast, the 2020s series, shown in red, sits lower on the chart, beginning the year below 14 million square kilometers and dropping to approximately 4.5 million square kilometers in September. The black line representing 2025 tracks closely with the 2020s average, starting near 13 million square kilometers and following a similar trajectory to the recent decadal mean. Across all decades, the lines converge most tightly during the winter months of January through March, while the vertical gap between the 1980s and the 2020s widens significantly during the summer melt season, particularly in August and September. The lowest recorded values for every decade occur in month 9, where the 2020s and 2025 lines dip below the 5 million square kilometer mark. The visual arrangement of the lines shows a clear stratification, with each successive decade positioned below the previous one, illustrating a reduction in total ice area across the calendar year.
